speaker: interviewer
text: We're in Toronto, interviewing Dennis Urstein. And this tape four. Dennis, do you think being a survivor affected your relationship with your daughter in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. I talked to her when I thought that she would understand what it was all about. In other words, we didn't start when she was 3, 5, 7, 9 years old. I don't even remember the exact time when I talked to her about it. But uh, it did not-- no, I did not-- you know, there's so many stories. When one says, I didn't have it. So why should you, or something like that, which is wrong.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got a beautiful girl. That's-- what can I say? She is the greatest thing-- she's better than sliced bread and cake. And I love cake. She's great. She's got a good-- listen, she's got a good background, her mother. She can keep up with me. [LAUGHS] No, my daughter is beautiful. She lives in Los Angeles. How do you feel about being Canadian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the word fantastic is-- is-- is really too weak to express oneself. Uh, it's a country where you can do what you want. You can say what you want. It's not always-- they not always listen to you. I don't always agree with their judicial system, and especially what-- things that have to do with uh, war criminals. I think they are very weak in that respect. And I think that the US is much stronger that way. But, as they say, democracy has uh, two sides. One's sharp, and one's dull. And if you-- if you want to uh, use uh, the privileges that you get, then you have to take the good and the bad. Ah, to be here is-- this is my home. You know, I always get very angry when I speak to people that are immigrants, like me-- even if they came earlier, a year earlier, two years later, or five years later-- and they say, back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, back home where? On Bay Street? Or back home in Hungary. There is no-- my home is here. This is back home. What other home is there? I happen to be born there. I wish I wouldn't. I wish I would have been born here, so we've-- both of us, my wife and me, would have saved ourselves a lot of problems. But, uh, no, this is-- Canada is a great country. We don't-- as I said before, I don't always agree. But I guess that's human nature. Who always agrees with me? Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luck. Sheer luck. Nothing else but luck. Not smart. You know, there's-- there's a word that I use to myself-- anesthetized. I think I became-- and a lot of people did-- become anesthetized. And we also became invisible. And I know this is a-- a mouthful, where you say, how can you become invisible? But how I did it, I don't know. But eh-- I became invisible by having no eye contact. I became invisible because maybe I was hiding halfways behind something, either a human being or an implement, or I don't know what. But most of it was luck. And my grandfather. I believed in-- in him looking after me. [PAUSE] [LAUGHS] And he still does. I think so. And I-- he's got an extended family now. He's looking now not only after myself-- after my wife, after my daughter, after my granddaughter. He's a great guy. And ah, that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But uh, you know, when you say Canada, the life-- it's-- it's the family that counts. It's-- it's unfort-- the unfortunate part-- I always agree that there are some bad things. I have a sister, right? And I think that uh, after the age of 14, until now, we were never longer together than, I don't know-- together, I mean-- a week, or two, or three, or four, although, in England, we live in the same country. But she lived in London. We lived somewhere else.

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, and uh, I know when my wife, Claire-- we-- I call her Muki. Muki. Um she hears that, I have to be thankful for [PAUSE FOR 5 SECONDS] many things that she did for me and still does. I'm always crying. I'm a softy. And my daughter-- the same. She-- she understands me. She can talk back to me. My granddaughter has a life in front of her-- taking stage direction. Maybe she's going to be-- I told her the other day, if she's becoming a big shot, maybe she's going to get us some free tickets for a show. And uh, I'm happy to be alive. Life's good. We always, hopefully, remember the good things, not the bad things, because they're more important. You can't dwell on these things. Although I do. And I can't help it. It's-- it's like the guy says, stop smoking. And he lights up a cigar. You know, it-- that's another thing my wife hates, my smoking. So she bans me to Siberia on the balcony. And at 24 below, or 23 below 0 Celsius, on a balcony, you smoke very short cigarettes. Because it's cold. But I still do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, no, it-- So you're full of contradictions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am. Of course. I always was. She calls me something else, but-- and she's right. Why are you smiling now? That's the truth. She calls me something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop here and-- and have Claire join you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Claire Urstein. I was born on February 14, 1922, in Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden-- maiden name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maid-- maiden name was Kimerling, K-I-M-E-R-L-I-N-G-- forgotten 49 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we're still married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, as you know, we going to have our wedding anniversary in nine days.

speaker: interviewee
text: 49 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are married twice. We were married on Friday the 13 and on Saturday the 14th. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the-- there were no synagogues. So we have to be married--

speaker: interviewee
text: Religiously.

speaker: interviewee
text: In hu-- in our home, in Austria. And uh, at-- and then at the registrar office.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had two wedding parties.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had the wedding party for all my Jewish friends that were in camp with me, in a D-- they were in a DP camp down there. And then we had a uh-- a celebration the next day. And both parties were roaring drunks, including the rabbi. As a matter of fact, and he-- she's laughing because it's the truth. When my boys-- you know, in the beginning, they didn't recognize her-- they gave me hell for going around with her. Because she didn't look Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got hell from all my friends. Used to say, how can you marry her? Didn't they do enough against you? They killed you at [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, she's Jewish. What do you mean? She-- she doesn't look like a Jew. So I had to bring her birth certificate that was issued by the ah, Jewish community in Vienna, you know, and show that to them. And then, OK. Then they made the wedding. They brought the rabbi. And the first thing that the guy said when he brought him in-- he says, don't give him a drink. I said, why? He says, because he's going to get drunk and he won't do the ceremony. But he did, anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was really nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was beautiful. She had to walk around me, what, seven times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you want to know what-- during the war, what happened to me? Ah, I was two years in the labor camp, which I more or less escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The labor camp was in-- in Germany. And ah, I went back to Vienna. I took the David star down, certainly, when I went on a train. But because of my looks, I was not in trouble. I ah, came to Vienna, phoned a friend. And she said, you come here immediately. And ah, from there on, formed a group which were all anti-Hitler. And ah, more or less, I slept one night, two nights-- you couldn't stay too long in a home. So for three years, I was hidden. But not hidden like I found out after the war, that there were 800 people in Vienna hidden in at-- in attics, and in cellars, and everywhere. But I was very daring. I was young. And I ah, stayed-- for three years, people helped me that way. And ah, I saw it through. And then I went to a place in Austria called Salzburg, which-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --where I met Dennis, after a short while. And we, uh-- I don't know if you talked about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, go ahead. You tell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We met in May. [LAUGHS] It sounds like that-- we met in May, engaged in June, and got married in September. Now, after the war, you know, everything went very fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, uh, I chased her until she caught me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm. Because you know why? Um, she-- she married a baby. I'm two years younger than she is. She married a youthful, good-looking--

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't be so excited.

speaker: interviewee
text: --guy. I beg your pardon. I'm not going to get excited. It's the truth!

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was very fashionable then. Today, women marry men who are much younger. Married already 49 years ago--

speaker: interviewee
text: She started early.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a younger man, which is fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sometimes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Boy, that takes-- did you-- did you-- did you notice, it took at least five seconds until she said yes? Your daughter is going to have fun when she sees that tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she will. We miss her. We terrible miss her that she lives in Los Angeles. But with-- we thank god we go there once or twice a year. She comes here. And ah, so we keep our-- our love and our-- it's tremendous, if you only have one child. Her daughter lives in Toronto. But she's going on 24. And she's ah--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they have to live their own lives. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of your granddaughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eliza. Eliza Hoff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband talks all the time about the war. Do you think very much about that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. That period didn't happen. I-- I pushed it out. I just don't. Which he-- mind you, what he went through can-- I cannot even-- when my labor camp was not even-- was very close to Buchenwald, where I was. And uh--

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aschersleben. But it was just this town, the city. Well, no. No city-- it was very small. I worked in a paper factory. They made-- they made also big maps for the army, which was a very secretive work. Certainly we worked not on that. We made boxes. And we started at 5:00 or 6:00 in the morning and uh, worked through till 6:00 at night. We had-- we had bunk beds and uh, straw-filled mat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mattresses.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had something where our-- we had a woman who was a very, very big anti-Semite. And she-- when she was rough, she did hit us. And um, it was-- but it was not-- I didn't bec-- again, I was 17 years old, or 18. And I ah-- I saved my life. Because my relatives who were left in Vienna were all taken. And my father was taken in '39. And my mother went to-- to England in '39, and my sister. So I was alone after that. I had to bring myself up.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have lived in a lot of fear.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never occurred to me. I was so sure. I wish I had all this confidence now. Which I-- yeah, I have, really. It's not too bad. Because-- but it's different when you're young. Your-- your fear's entirely-- it's-- it's not-- it doesn't exist-- didn't exist. Where I told Dennis there, just a few days ago, when we had the workers there from other countries--

speaker: interviewee
text: Foreign workers.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Foreign laborers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Foreign laborers. And it-- everything was blacked out. There was no light in the streets. But they opened the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Man hole.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the man-- man hole. The people, if they were out, they would fall in there. That was somehow there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sabotage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sabotage. I-- I-- but I don't know. There was somebody, like Dennis's grandfather, somebody looking after me. And if you have that confidence, or that-- that-- it was-- it's not confidence. It's just you believe in something. I also went to pray to a place where I would not normally go, because nothing-- no synagogues were there. And God hears you anywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she still believes in it. We don't have a dispute about it. But she does have her own ways of thinking about it. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. You-- you have to hold on to something. But I really-- like I say, it did not occupy me too much. I just to go through the day. Only once I was in ah--

speaker: interviewee
text: Trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in trouble. I saw a neighbor, where we lived as a family, before Hitler. And he spotted me. And he would have straight gone. I had no papers. And in Vienna, they walked around-- the police-- and ah, and they says-- and ask your papers. I had no papers. I couldn't afford to buy them. You could buy them. They were anywhere I would say $2,000. I had no money. But when I saw him, I run. That was the only time I had run. And the other time, when Vienna-- when we were liberated in Vienna, and the Russian came in-- that was my biggest fear, I must say. There was fear. Because we lived 10 days in this-- in the cellar, from the buildings, because shooting around Vienna. And liberate-- the liberation took that long. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: The people who saved you-- they were not Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: None.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- were they all people you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They knew-- knew each other. And one was telling the other, could this girl sleep a day or two? I worked for a family who was an aristocrat, as it happened, and his wife, and their two children. And I worked there as a nanny, more or less. And they shared whatever food they could get, because everything was on ration. It was very little. So I slept on the floor. It-- it was as long as you don't in the night, in the street, to be. Because that was most dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about Austrians?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if the word hate-- I highly dislike them. You mean the people? Ah, highly dislike them, especially in-- well, the ones who saved me in Vienna, I mean-- I didn't even say I like them. But I know they help me. But in Salzburg, where I met Dennis, it's a very-- place very famous for being very anti-Jewish, anti--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anti-Semitic. Very bad place. So uh, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know the uh-- I just want to add one thing in there. With all the people that we met, everyone-- we met a Nazi. He used to save one Jew. The other one saved one Jew. We came to the conclusion that for every Jew that he saved, OK, it's not that 6 millions got killed. There should be another 12 millions around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was their excuse. Everybody said, I-- my best friend this. My best friend that. I saved him here. And I saved her here. I don't believe in it. Because I have proof.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but like I say, it's not-- I don't think about it at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any final comments to make about your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: About my husband. Well, I'm glad we found each other and be together, for another few years. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] It's all right, sweetheart. [SIGHS] The inevitable is going to happen. When it's happens, it happens. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: As long as our daughter is OK, and our granddaughter is OK, hey.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope we make it to our 50th next year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that would be a good idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're all invited.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said. You too-- the cameramen. Everybody. [PAUSE FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, this tattoo was done ah, at the time when I was supposed to ah, be gassed. And so I had to put that-- I put that on. The first letter is L, for Leon. And the second is M, for Marie. That's my mother-- my mother and father. Then there is D and L, for Dolfy and Lily. And the date on the bottom is the 22nd of April, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the significance of the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the day that my parents got killed. That's when were murdered. [PAUSE FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, this is my number that I got when I arrived in Auschwitz, on August 14, 1942. The number is 622-- ah, oh, boy! 62022. 62,022. And this is a picture of my uncle, father of my brother, and his sister, which my sister found somewhere. It's ah, of the 20th of June, 1905.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Solomon and Frania.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, his name was Urstein. That was my ah-- my uncle. And her name was Perlmutter. Ah, this is a picture of my family, taken in 1936-- my father, my sister Lily, myself, and my mother Marie. And I was ah, 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon. It was taken in Vienna. Ah, this is me in the ah, US army-- my uniform. 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: 21. They called me Errol Flynn. This was the US soldier that I-- oh, befriended me, in Dachau, 1945, approximately two days after liberation-- so it could be ah, the second of May-- who brought me all the chocolates and who made me feel like a human being. Because he was the first person that really did a good deed for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul. That's all I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what unit he was in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. Well, he must-- he must have been in the unit that liberated ah, Dachau. I-- And that's our wedding picture. Look how happy we are. September 14th, 1946. The honeymoon just started. We're still on our honeymoon. The whole family, 1987. Marilyn, my daughter. Claire, my wife. Eliza, my granddaughter. And the big D, me. This is a more recent picture of my daughter Marilyn, myself, and my wife Claire, at a reunion of the ah, Holocaust. Eliza, my granddaughter, at her high school graduation. This is Otto Graf, the SS man that-- from Auschwitz, that we used to call the [GERMAN], the meter reader. He was the one that brought the gas, the Zyklon B, to the crematoriums when a transport arrived. And he was put on trial in 1972, because he not only did that-- we saw him kill several people. But unfortunately, he got off. And this is ah, Heinz Creneman. That's the latest one, the opera singer, that ah, beat a young Dutch boy by name of Joop to death, because he brought some food to some women prisoners that worked with us in the same Kommando. He went on trial. In 1991 he won, and again in 1992, because some new evidence came out. And he ah, was also-- oh, his case was closed. Because he had a so-called, quote, heart ailment. And this is a certificate of appreciation from a-- the Bramalea Secondary School that I have ah, talked to young people for the last three or four years. And ah, it makes me feel good to receive something like that. Ah, this is the ah, transportation list that was published by Beate and Serge Klarsfeld about the transports that left Belgium and ah, went to Auschwitz. And as you can see, you have my name on there, Adolf, which is my real name, and Leibusch, which is actually Leon. That's my father. And Marie, which is my mother. The cap that I took from the SS man that we executed in Dachau. Ah, I must note that the word Dachau came from somebody's dress uniform sleeve. So I just cut it out and put it on there. I wanted to keep this as a mement-- memento.
